Something that we have the concept of dynamic policies, which most of our competition does not have.

And that's kind of a, as an organization, most of our competitors just set up a static policy, whereas ours is dynamic policy, which means that if, for example, you're going to San Francisco in a dull time of the year, then rather than paying the premium amount, then the policy reduces the amount that people could book.
That also helps to the company's kind of savings.
That's, I assume, if I'm not mistaken, Libby, are one of the only ones that offers that in the market that differentiates us.
Yeah, there's that.

policy cap, you could also get rewards to then book personal travel.

So we not only offer travel to business travelers, but also you can get great rates as just a consumer, a regular consumer.
Which helps with adoption.
Yes.
Yeah, and that's the real reason for it.

So when you think about the market and who you're competing against, right, and the perception that you want to have on these different parts of your business, like the support, but honestly overall, like how you want to bond to be positioned, where that is today, where you want it to be six months, a year from now.
Like, is there many progress you want to make in some differentiators against your competitors that we can help get there through content?
Yeah, I think one of the challenges that we have is that we are an indie adopter of AI.
So we have been using AI, our own, we have our own kind of product that is basically the brain of the whole system that is basically AI-driven.
So,

So we were, we have been ahead of the game for a long time, but now some of this competition is, are basically starting talking about all these AI features that we've had for two or three years ago, for at least two or three years.

But we haven't been talking about them in the same language, so they might come across more AI-friendly and AI-forward than we are because they are having more recent conversations around it, whereas they talk about stuff that we've had in our product for a few years, right?
So I think kind of embedding the AI conversations and the features and functionalities that we have, I think would be one way to kind of make sure that we are seen as a leader.
Yeah, that's helpful.
It's volume as well as language, right?
It's like who you want to be speaking to, what you want to be speaking about.
As well as increasing the volume of noise, because it sounds like you already have a lot of the features, the capability in place.
It's more like, who do you want to communicate this to, and what's the strategy to get the volume up to a number that's going to start making a meaningful impact?

I think when it comes to competitors, too, another thing to keep in mind is that there's these legacy competitors, like Concur or Agencia, as Nima mentioned before, and our buyers often are going to just want to go with them because they're the safe choice.
They're the ones that feel trustworthy.
They've been around forever.
So there's hesitance to make a switch to the more tech-forward, AI-enabled types of technology, like Navan.
So that's another thing we're constantly trying to convince people of or combat.
Yeah.
That's the...
Another thing is that some of the people who are actually making these decisions in the larger companies are travel managers.
And these are people who have been in the same role for, I don't know, 15, 20 years.
And then so they have kind of a way of doing things and maybe they're just about to go and retire in a couple of years.
They don't want to rock the boats.
And then especially given the fact that from on the other side, we also talk about AI, there is that fear of, is this going to replace my role?
Kind of that's another fear factor that those buyers might have that would impact the decision-making process as well.
How are you overcoming those objections for them now?
Rich has been writing a lot of good content around that topic to kind of get some visibility into.

So we have some collaterals already that Rich has produced, and it's more about the career arc of a travel manager and how they need to think through their career and how they should be considered as the top leader in their organization rather than just a legacy product owner.

It's 300 and under, and that's where most of our content has been produced around that as well as the end user.
But then my team is responsible for upper market, which is 300 and up.
Of course, the objective is the larger the company, the better for us.
But of course, with search, it's a little bit harder to do that.

But the type of content that we could produce, if, for example, it talks about the mergers and acquisitions, impact on travel management, or if it talks about the travel managers itself, that lends itself to keywords that the more enterprise-sized companies care about.
Because if you're a SMB or even a commercial account, you probably don't have a dedicated travel manager job function.
Whereas if you are in a big enough of a company, then that's worthwhile having a dedicated resource for it, right?
But in SMB, it could be run by finance.
It could be run by procurement, could be run by HR, and so on and so forth.
So there's no dedicated.
So identifying what are the keywords that actually help us show up for upper market is going to be a big part of this project.
Yeah, this is helpful.
Thank you.

so as we go build all this research and the strategy, we're happy to incorporate any findings or learnings or other goals that we've set out into and including in strategy.

Where is Navan losing in deals to competitors?

think on the travel, if we have the right champions and people who are bought into the concept of modernized technology, we win at a really good rate.
I think the challenge is when you are not able to find a champion or they are not necessarily open to change or open to take a risk.
Because in some cases, they have also burned by some of these legacy ones.
So, for example, I was on a sales cycle on one of these large accounts.
And they said, oh, we're not going

I take it on because it takes a lot of IT resources because in the past, for example, they've used one of our competitions, FCM, to implement and it took them a thousand hours to implement the product.

And as a result, they said, okay, we're not going to go through another implementation of a thousand hours because we do not have enough IT resources and we have other AI projects on the roadmap that we want to work.
Whereas in reality, the implementation of our product, it takes less than a hundred hours.
So they had overestimated by 10x how much it costs to kind of implement our product.
So that's if we are not in the conversation with the right people because the larger accounts, there is a lot of different personas.
And that one, the travel manager, the finance, everybody was on board, but the IT chem said, I do not have the resources and it's costing me a thousand hours to do it.
Right.
That was not real assumptions, but because we haven't conveyed that message well enough, they came up with that kind of a pushback.
But when it comes to expense, the story is a little bit different, because our expense product is another standalone expense product.
If we go to a competition just for expense, we have challenges winning the deals.
We are much more successful on that front when it's a travel and expense.

And in that case, it's actually giving us an edge because it's the same product, whereas when, for example, Sunker is different from Agencia, so those two technologies do not talk to each other, right?
One thing that goes back to our differentiation, we also have this analytics tool that provides a lot of visibility to today.
And travel managers and finance that it takes long hours of work to get those type of data from Concord, for example, right?
These are kind of the, if you're able to kind of inflate the challenges, you have a good chance of winning.
But anybody else wants to add anything else?
I think you covered it pretty well, Nima.
So I've been reading all your content, so that's fine.
Then what's maybe like a common trigger for people to kind of get into this review cycle of looking at either travel or travel and expense with Navan?
Is it the legacy players that, like, pain points of their systems not talking to each other?
Is it just their travel, like TMC company they currently have, can't scale with them?
Can you just talk to a little bit about other triggers that might be leading to consideration?
Yeah, so there are a few things I could think of.
One is new people joining a company who have used more modern technologies in the past, like Navan, and they see the current one doesn't do the same, so that's one element.
The other one is, for example, I use the concept of this CWT who was just acquired, right?
So now that you're going through an acquisition, usually there is a cause of, oh, let me see if I have to change or migrate or things like that.
Or should I look at the whole thing from scratch again?
Or in some cases...
Yeah.
Although...
Oh, Because think
They try to kind of optimize one part of the business or they have the kind of cost-saving kind of approaches they want to have and that's another why they might be starting it.
But this is a sort of an investment because once somebody makes the decision, they usually stay with it for a long time because it happens very often.

The other, sometimes, at least when I was at Kong, that's what drove the initiative was that because all the employees were so fed up with not being able to find the flights that they wanted to have, that they didn't have enough kind of the saving and stuff like that.
And then all the employees were complaining to our procurement team who was owning our agency out, okay, this is just wasting my time, I can get things cheaper, better.
And a better timeline and everything somewhere else.
So we made so much noise as employees that they had to kind of start the search again.
So sometimes also driven by the end user complaints, but percentage-wise, I don't know which one of these ones are driving.
Anything else, Tim, that I'm not thinking of?
No, I just was reminded, Nima, though, earlier this year, we put a name to the last thing you mentioned.

We called it the screenshot problem, and it's the fact that people will often find a cheaper flight or booking outside of their company's TMC, and then they'll take a screenshot of it and send it to their travel manager or whomever is running the program.
And, you know, if that happens enough times, it can be a good catalyst to them realizing they should probably seek out other options.
Mm-hmm.
Thank you very Thank you.
Eric, anything else here on the customer?
we move down to kind of what's working in the current state?
Yep, I so.

We kind of covered some objections or concerns.

Yeah, I think here is looking into so much and we can see the numbers, but would love to understand maybe less about what the process and how it works, but what you all think are like fantastic pieces of anchor content or, hey, if I had 20 or 30 more consistent flow of these types of blogs or articles, like I'd be extremely happy.
Like, what is something that you've done or something that the companies put out that you're really happy with to kind of help us understand that quality bar?
I will say that one thing we have coming out soon is this analysis that Forrester did on Navan where they dug up a bunch of cost savings through various aspects of our platform.
And where that ties in nicely is that we did another survey that Forrester published as well where we found that cost is the top concern of people switching platforms.
It's not necessarily disruption, but it's cost, which has always been a concern, but is now the has become the top concern this year.
So we've got we've got some really good data backing up.
So, you know, why some of these concerns over switching, why they are not as important as they could be.
Some other things we've seen perform well, excuse me, SEO-wise, there's a couple pieces on new distribution capability that have done really well.
Mainly people probably trying to just understand what it is, but be some startups that it does well consistently.
And some of our other content that's performed well in the past on, like, social media, a lot of our, like, data stories.
So anything that's tracking business travel trends, we have a whole wealth of data at our fingertips here at Nevada.
That we could tap into more, so that might be an area that we can dig in on.
Yeah, I think those are probably the top, but something I can also think on and give back to you guys with some more details.
I don't know if Moran, there's anything you've seen in your six months here that you'd want to mention.

So we do have some traffic generators, but I feel that in many cases, even if we have amazing rankings with a keyword such as, I don't know, business travel management software where we can rank on first, but we would not see too many clicks coming out of those searches at the end of the day.
And I feel very keen on content that converts well.
End of the day and produces business.

And while we are seeing some content that has been generating traffic, especially top of funnel type of educational content ones, some glossaries, some blogs, some comparative blogs, they do not tend to convert down the line.
And as much as I would recognize from previous companies I worked for, for example, so I don't really have a good answer to that.
Yeah, I don't have much answer there, but I would say I have a wish list and it's like coming up with the bottom of funnel content that, as Mara mentioned, would transconvert to, track.
And that could be in the form of jobs to be done that different personas care about.
So I would love to get very technical.
Okay, if you are a CPO, chief, what does CPO stand for?
No, procurement, procurement, thank you, chief procurement officer.

Or if you are a head of finance and stuff like that, based on, or if you're a travel manager, based on each roles, what they need to do on a day-to-day basis, and what would be the problems they're trying to solve.
And that way, we would be able to drive content that would not only drive traffic, but also convert standard and funnel.
That's amazing.

And my eyes lit up because that's one of the processes we run here is artifacts we create after we do a ton of research, our persona documents and artifacts, and so we take in all the persona information that you have.

But then we also build out by role and by type, like what does their day look like, what motivates them, what frustrates them, what do they struggle with in their day-to-day job, and like understanding all the little nuances of how this person actually operates, right, it enables us to create content and answer the questions that we actually care about.

And a lot of that ends up lower in the funnel, but we can create content at each stage of the funnel for that person, so we can get really, really, really tactical and focused about it, so completely aligned.
Perfect, that's what you're looking for.

I know you mentioned there's certain content top of funnel, and it's not converting very well, even some of the comparison stuff, but where would you say your best leads and users are coming from today?
Apparently, the majority of the leads we get are just product sign-ups, or people who try to sign up for the product and we push them through the...
Request a demo.
So that's where we get the majority of the leads.
Of course, the way our website was designed was not designed as such where it drives traffic to white papers or e-books or a lot of other type of CTAs in the past.
